title: Reading and Use of English – Part 1
description: Read the text below and decide which answer (A, B, C or D) best fits each gap.

content.articleTitle: The Cultural Impact of the Spice Trade
content.text: For centuries, the spice trade was the primary engine of global exploration, (0) driving sailors and merchants across uncharted waters in search of rare seasonings. Spices like cinnamon, cloves, and pepper were once (1) more than their weight in gold, sparking fierce competition among European powers. This (2) for profit led to the establishment of vast maritime empires and the discovery of new territories. However, the trade was about more than just wealth; it (3) a significant role in the exchange of ideas and cultures. As ships (4) from port to port, they carried not only goods but also religious beliefs, linguistic influences, and culinary traditions. This cross-cultural interaction (5) the development of modern global society. Despite the dangers of the journey, the potential rewards were high enough to (6) the risks involved. Over time, the monopoly held by certain nations began to (7) as new routes were discovered and trade became more accessible. Today, while spices are common household items, their history (8) as a reminder of the era when a simple peppercorn could change the course of human history and redefine the maps of the world, connecting distant civilizations in ways that were previously completely and utterly unimaginable.

example.explanation: “Driving” in this context means being the primary force or motivation behind an action.

questions.explanation: “Worth more than” is the standard comparative structure for value. “Valued” would require “at” or a different sentence structure.
“Quest for profit” is a common literary and historical collocation describing a long and difficult pursuit.
“Played a role” is the fixed idiomatic expression. The other verbs are not used with 'role'.
“Sailed” is the most contextually appropriate verb for maritime trade in a historical context.
“Shaped the development” is a very common academic collocation for historical influence.
“Justify the risks” is a standard collocation meaning to provide a good enough reason for taking a danger.
“Crumble” is used here figuratively to describe the slow collapse of a system or monopoly.
“Serves as a reminder” is a fixed formal collocation.
